paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
tei_path: /content/drive/MyDrive/content_analysis_epistemology/papers/tei/raw/psychology/Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin.tei.xml

metadata.title: Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes: A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccination

abstract: In this study, we analyzed >200,000 posts collected from Facebook public pages that were published in December 2020 during the rollout of the first dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech vaccine to the American public. We ran both topic modeling and sentiment analysis of the posts and found that first Facebook posts talked about not only treatment effectiveness such as trial results and testing approaches but also other issues that surround vaccines such as approval and distribution. Second, although the general sentiment during this period was positive and anticipation was the highest emotion, Facebook posts expressed a salient amount of fear and sadness, especially when discussing emergency approval and trial results. Finally, we found that both topics and sentiments have a significant influence on user likes. Using the topic of vaccine distribution as a baseline, posts that discuss related aspects of vaccines (e.g., effectiveness, shipment, and testing), call for actions (e.g., use of masks) and indicate care to vulnerable groups (e.g., health care workers and seniors) received more likes. Posts with bad news (e.g., new cases and deaths) and doubts over the usefulness of vaccines received fewer likes. Regardless of their valences, approach emotions lead to more likes whereas withdrawal emotions lead to fewer likes. Our study suggests that to facilitate actions, using certain topics and approach emotions in the posts could be helpful.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
paragraphs: T he World Health Organization listed vaccine hes- itancy as one of the 10 top threats to global health in 2019. 1 In the wake of COVID-19, this threat has been exacerbated by the politicization of the pandemic by prominent public figures, and further compounded by the ubiquitous presence of social media platforms, which have emerged as major arbiters of information access for ordinary citizens. Thus, there is a need to better characterize online discourses surrounding sensitive and highly politicized issues such as the COVID-19 vaccine, as a first step to understand how they might be affecting ordinary users. Prior efforts have largely focused on Twitter, [2][3][4][5] thanks to the platform's relatively open application programming interface (API).

However, if we want to fully use social media as an approach to social listening, 6 we have to listen comprehensively because not all social media platforms are the same. Facebook has been the most popular social media platform for more than a decade, 7 but studies using Facebook data to examine COVID-19 vaccines are still rare. 8 In addition, using social media to listen is often limited by its observational nature. 9 However, it still allows for the derivation of correlational relationships between various factors and engagement metrics. 10 The observations of social media voices are linked to either historical events or geographical boundaries. 4,5 Other analyses link sources to user attention, 3 or content features to user sharing. 9 Our study relies on >200,000 posts collected from public Facebook pages to analyze the topics and sentiments found in the posts, and links topics and sentiments to user reactions such as likes.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
paragraphs: Agenda-setting theory argues that exposure to certain issues will increase the salience of these issues in the public's minds. 11 Social media such as Facebook and Twitter have become important ecosystems where the issue agenda is set for the public. However, social media have challenged this classic theory in at least three ways: first, who sets the agenda has been expanded to include not only media professionals such as journalists and editors, but also nonmedia actors and platform algorithms. Several recent studies on Facebook and COVID-19 vaccines have raised concerns about anti-vax groups and misinformation spreaders who used alternative narratives to set their own, 12,13 although controversial, agenda on Facebook. Other scholars of social media agenda setting have speculated about the potential of platform algorithms to prioritize certain issues over others. 14 Second, the issues on the agenda are highly diverse due to the diverse user base of social media. Facing information overload, users increasingly rely on their social networks to filter out the issues they need to pay attention to. 15 Third, because of the diverse issues and agenda setters as well as the networks formed among them, users have a higher possibility of incidentally encountering information. 15 To help answer these theoretical challenges, our article follows the network agenda setting (NAS) model to update the concept of issues, by defining the topics identified using unsupervised topic modeling as subissues. 16 The NAS model, unlike the classic model, asserts that the agenda of the media is both implicit and explicit. Not only do the explicit mentions of issues in one message but also other issues mentioned in other messages during the same period influence the salience of issues in public perception. Following this logic, we redefine the agenda issue as a dynamic network of subissues, implicitly and concurrently expressed in social media messages. Topic modeling delineates a target number of topics from messages, while considering all potential topics simultaneously.

Therefore, topics are not absolute mentions of subissues, but relative probabilities in view of other subissues. Topic modeling starts with the corpus on which the algorithm is meant to run. Choosing the corpus using a set of relevant keywords already defines what the broad issue is-namely, the COVID-19 vaccine rollout. Therefore, the topics identified by running topic modeling on this corpus does not identify other broad issues in the public discourse on Facebook, but rather, the subissues under this broad issue. Conceptually, subissues represent a layer of agenda setting that still falls under the first-layer agenda setting, 17 but distinguishes itself by drawing user attention to specific aspects of a complex issue. By adding this level of subissues, the NAS model can be expanded to include the analyses of relationships among subissues, which build the foundation on which issue networks can be empirically formed. 16 The second-layer agenda-setting theory further suggests that how issues are talked about, that is, content features, will influence public perceptions. 17 Seen in this light, sentiments can be interpreted as content features that can alter how the content is received. A literature survey of scholarship that has focused on emotion theories to understand the importance of sentiments reveals two ways of classifying emotions: the valence + arousal model that emphasizes pleasant and unpleasant feelings as well as the intensity shown through physiological signals 18 ; the motivation model that emphasizes approach and withdrawal emotions that have different directions of facilitating versus inhibiting actions. 19 The valence+arousal model sees emotion as an appraisal process. 20 When an individual forms the appraisal of an object or event, those patterns of appraisals lead to certain states of positive/negative feelings and physiological changes, which influence corresponding perceptions and behaviors. In contrast, the motivation model sees emotions as brain activities that do not have to go through the brain areas that are responsible for cognitive appraisals, and instead correspond to the areas that directly account for taking action.
Although each emotion has its own unique motivational functions and behavioral associations, they can be broadly classified into approach emotions and withdrawal emotions.

Approach emotions are defined as emotions that lead to greater left hemisphere activation, motivating people to take action to achieve a goal. Withdrawal emotions are defined as emotions that lead to greater right hemisphere activation, demotivating people from taking action. Although earlier sentiment analysis studies often consider sentiments using the valence+arousal model, 18,21 there are studies suggesting that the appraisal process is hard to establish through examining the sentiments. 20 With the advancement of sentiment analysis tools, discrete types of emotions can now be identified. Our study thus examines both the valences and discrete emotions in our analyses, to verify which emotion theory is more suitable for social media analyses.

Considering the importance of social media content in influencing the perceived salience of issues among the public, the first set of research questions we pose are as follows: What are the topics and sentiments seen in the Facebook posts? How are the topics related to sentiments? Social media reactions such as likes are indicators of attention-paying, 3 which eventually may lead to perceived salience of the issues. We thus further ask: How do the topics and sentiments influence likes? Are posts with certain topics/sentiments more liked than others?

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
paragraphs: A total of 209,793 posts were collected from Facebook public pages archived by CrowdTangle, a data source increasingly used by researchers. 22,23 The posts were published between December 11-18, 2020, during the FDA authorization and subsequent rollout of the first dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine. When using the CrowdTangle API to search through its database, we included all post types in the English Language, regardless of whether the post is branded content or not. We only included verified accounts because we are interested in authentic content by legitimate users. We only focused on posts that were published by pages administered in the United States. The process of searching posts is specified in Supplementary Table S1, with search criteria, the full list of keywords and their validation.

paragraphs: To preprocess the text, we first removed URLs and punctuation. Using Gensim and Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) libraries in Python, we tokenized the texts to divide them into words and removed all stopwords. We then reduced all words to their root forms by stemming and lemmatizing.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
paragraphs: For topic modeling, we combined the ''Message,'' ''Image Text,'' and ''Description'' columns in the CrowdTangle data set and applied Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to extract 12 topics (Supplementary Fig. S1). The optimal number of topics was decided by estimating the coherence scores of the resultant models for numbers ranging from 2 to 30, and identifying the value corresponding to the highest coherence score. The inter-topic distance was visualized in Python using the pyLDAvis package to show how similar the topics are (Supplementary Fig. S2). Based on topic similarity, we reran a final model with 10 distinct topics. To test robustness, we ran the LDA model on each of the three text columns, namely ''Message,'' ''Image Text,'' and ''Description.'' In our topic model, most of the qualitative topics were preserved compared with those derived from the individual columns. In other words, the combination of the three text columns did not result in the exclusion of any qualitative topics.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
paragraphs: We performed sentiment analysis by using two lexiconbased tools, both of which have been used in prior studies. The first was VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and Sentiment Reasoner), a lexicon and rule-based sentiment analysis tool that is specifically attuned to sentiments expressed in social media. 24 We used it to capture the polarity of sentiments. By calculating the ratios for proportions of text that fall in each category, it generated a compound score by summing the valence scores of each word in the lexicon and then normalized it to the range -1 (extremely negative) to 1 (extremely positive). This value for each post was then used to classify the post as either positive (score >0), neutral (score = 0), or negative (score <0).

Next, the National Research Council (NRC) lexicon was used to identify specific emotions expressed in posts. The NRC Emotion Lexicon provides a list of English words as well as their associations with eight basic human emotions: anger, fear, anticipation, trust, surprise, sadness, joy, and disgust. 25 A post is assigned an emotion category based on which of these emotion scores is highest. Posts were assigned to both categories if they were tied for the highest value. We combined our topic modeling and sentiment analysis results to explore the emotional type of posts belonging to different topics, to understand if certain topics were associated with specific emotions.
sections.text:
We performed sentiment analysis by using two lexiconbased tools, both of which have been used in prior studies. The first was VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and Sentiment Reasoner), a lexicon and rule-based sentiment analysis tool that is specifically attuned to sentiments expressed in social media. 24 We used it to capture the polarity of sentiments. By calculating the ratios for proportions of text that fall in each category, it generated a compound score by summing the valence scores of each word in the lexicon and then normalized it to the range -1 (extremely negative) to 1 (extremely positive). This value for each post was then used to classify the post as either positive (score >0), neutral (score = 0), or negative (score <0).

Next, the National Research Council (NRC) lexicon was used to identify specific emotions expressed in posts. The NRC Emotion Lexicon provides a list of English words as well as their associations with eight basic human emotions: anger, fear, anticipation, trust, surprise, sadness, joy, and disgust. 25 A post is assigned an emotion category based on which of these emotion scores is highest. Posts were assigned to both categories if they were tied for the highest value. We combined our topic modeling and sentiment analysis results to explore the emotional type of posts belonging to different topics, to understand if certain topics were associated with specific emotions.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
We performed sentiment analysis by using two lexiconbased tools, both of which have been used in prior studies. The first was VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and Sentiment Reasoner), a lexicon and rule-based sentiment analysis tool that is specifically attuned to sentiments expressed in social media. 24 We used it to capture the polarity of sentiments. By calculating the ratios for proportions of text that fall in each category, it generated a compound score by summing the valence scores of each word in the lexicon and then normalized it to the range -1 (extremely negative) to 1 (extremely positive). This value for each post was then used to classify the post as either positive (score >0), neutral (score = 0), or negative (score <0).

Next, the National Research Council (NRC) lexicon was used to identify specific emotions expressed in posts. The NRC Emotion Lexicon provides a list of English words as well as their associations with eight basic human emotions: anger, fear, anticipation, trust, surprise, sadness, joy, and disgust. 25 A post is assigned an emotion category based on which of these emotion scores is highest. Posts were assigned to both categories if they were tied for the highest value. We combined our topic modeling and sentiment analysis results to explore the emotional type of posts belonging to different topics, to understand if certain topics were associated with specific emotions.

By calculating the ratios for proportions of text that fall in each category, it generated a compound score by summing the valence scores of each word in the lexicon and then normalized it to the range -1 (extremely negative) to 1 (extremely positive).

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
paragraphs: The third analysis we ran aims to infer the influence of sentiments and topics on user likes. Since our dependent variable (i.e., the number of likes) was a count variable with significant overdispersion, we used negative binomial regressions (NBRs). We used the MASS package in R, and controlled for page-level fixed effects and other covariates such as the number of followers. The standard errors and 95 percent confidence intervals were also recalculated to account for within-page clustering. We ran two sets of models: the first had the dominant topic of each post (i.e., the topic with the highest probability) as the independent variable whereas the second had the probability of the post having each of the eight sentiments as the independent variable. Estimating these models allowed us to capture the extent to which the engagement received by the posts was contingent on the primary topic of the post, as well as its emotional content.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
sections.header_raw: Topics emerging from the COVID-19 vaccine posts
sections.header_norm: topics emerging from the covid 19 vaccine posts
paragraphs: LDA allowed us to identify 10 distinct topics in our corpus. Figure 1 illustrates the top 10 keywords by weight for each topic. Along with keywords, we derived the qualitative interpretation for each topic from the top 10 representative texts on that topic (Supplementary Table S2).

Topic 1 focused on the distribution of COVID-19 vaccines. It was closely related to a live report about how the COVID-19 vaccine will be distributed across the United States through Operation Warp Speed. Relatedly, Topic 5 centered around the actual shipment of the first dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech vaccine. Topic 2 concerned the trial effect of the vaccine. Topic 3 pertained to the FDA's emergency approval of the COVID-19 vaccine. Throughout the two topics' representative texts, mixed attitudes and opinions were expressed in regard to the following vaccination.

Topic 4 covered newly diagnosed cases and deaths. Topic 8 referred to COVID-19 testing approaches, including rapid tests in drugstores and first home testing kits. Topic 6 demonstrated calls for the use of masks by nongovernmental organizations (NGOs), especially schools and communities after the first dose was released. Topic 7 centered on the question of whether the vaccine could stop the pandemic. Topic 9 related to the news that senior government officials will be offered the vaccine in the first stance. Finally, topic 10 reported on the news about health care workers receiving vaccines.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
sections.header_raw: Sentiments emerging from the COVID-19 vaccine posts
sections.header_norm: sentiments emerging from the covid 19 vaccine posts
paragraphs: A descriptive analysis of sentiments identified in our corpus indicated a positive outlook toward the COVID-19 vaccine among Facebook public pages. As shown in Figure 2 With regard to topical content (Fig. 3), anticipation was most evident in the topics related to the shipment of the first dose of the Pfizer vaccine (Topic 5), NGO's call for wearing masks (Topic 6), and news about health care workers receiving vaccines (Topic 10). The highest levels of fear, trust, and sadness were expressed with regard to the emergency approval (Topic 3) and the trial effect (Topic 2) of the COVID-19 vaccine. In addition, fear was also demonstrated when discussing the government's plan to offer the vaccine to senior officials (Topic 9). The other topics were not coupled with such strong emotional expressions.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
sections.header_raw: The influence of topics and sentiments on likes
sections.header_norm: the influence of topics and sentiments on likes
paragraphs: Table 1 shows the NBR estimates and the incidence rate ratios of topics and sentiments on likes. Compared with posts with a dominant Topic 1 (vaccine distribution, which was the reference category), posts that have a dominant topic of 2 (the trial effect of the vaccine), 6 (calls for the use of masks), and 10 (health care workers receiving vaccines) received more than twice the number of likes (incidence rate ratio [IRR] = 2.17, 2.20, 2.54, respectively). Posts with a dominant topic of 5 (the actual shipment of the first dose), 7 (COVID-19 testing approaches), and 9 (senior government officials receiving vaccines) received more likes than topic 1, too. But the effect sizes were smaller, around 50 percent more likes. In contrast, posts with a dominant topic of 4 (newly diagnosed cases and deaths) and 8 (whether vaccines can stop the pandemic) received around 30 percent fewer likes than topic 1.

Unlike the expectation that positively valenced posts are more liked, our analysis shows no significant influence of overall valence on likes so we focused on discrete emotions. When we examine the eight sentiments, the results show that the motivation model works better in our data: Approach emotions, regardless of their valences, lead to more likes. In this study, we focus on the direction of coefficients because the high IRRs should be treated with caution. Although theoretically possible, it is unlikely that a post contains solely one emotion. Both positive emotions such as joy and negative ones such as anger facilitate immediate actions. In contrast, withdrawal emotions, regardless of their valences, lead to fewer likes. Fear, anticipation, and surprise all show a negative influence on likes because these emotions tend to inhibit immediate actions.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Topics and Sentiments Influence Likes A Study of Facebook Public Pages' Posts About COVID-19 Vaccin
paragraphs: Our study focuses on a critical moment in fighting COVID-19 and Facebook public pages' discussions about COVID-19 vaccines. During this important time window to shape first impressions about COVID-19 vaccines, we found that first, Facebook posts talked about not only treatment effectiveness such as trial results and testing approaches but also other issues that surround vaccines such as approval and distribution. Second, although the general sentiment during this period was positive and anticipation was the highest emotion, Facebook posts expressed a substantial amount of fear and sadness, especially when discussing emergency approval and trial results. Finally, we found that both topics and sentiments had a significant influence on user likes.

Using the topic of vaccine distribution as a baseline, posts that discussed related aspects of vaccines (e.g., effectiveness, shipment, and testing) have received more likes, probably because of the additional information they provide. Posts that call for actions (e.g., use of masks) and indicate care for vulnerable groups (e.g., health care workers and seniors) received more likes, too. Unsurprisingly, bad news (e.g., new cases and deaths) and doubts over the usefulness of vaccines received fewer likes. The overall valence of sentiments does not have as much influence as the motivational direction of sentiments when predicting likes. Regardless of their valences, approach emotions lead to more likes, whereas withdrawal emotions lead to fewer likes. This finding is particularly interesting in the context of COVID-19 vaccines. During this critical window when the anticipation of vaccines was high, ironically anticipation, a withdrawal emotion, only decreased likes, probably due to the uncertainty in anticipating something that is yet to happen.

Other than its practical implications, our article contributes to the theoretical expansion of the NAS model by taking a network perspective on both the first-layer and second-layer agenda setting. By redefining topics generated by topic modeling as subissues, we are able to introduce a conceptual tool to examine the additional level of the effect of subissues-how users engage with the content-that goes beyond simple issue salience. It takes into account the fact that one message can implicitly include multiple subissues and that user engagement with these messages is conditioned on the relative salience of 556 ZHANG ET AL. different subissues encoded in that message. Similarly, by treating sentiments as a second-layer agenda-setting feature, the NAS model is now able to understand the influence of sentiments in a dynamic and relative manner. In short, our analyses serve as the theoretical foundation on which the network nature of the NAS model can be better conceptualized and operationalized. We suggest that future studies on social media agenda setting should always take this network perspective in their analysis. In addition, our study helps us to theoretically understand the prediction of emotion theories in the context of social media. Traditionally, sentiments are understood as valences and their intensity. But our findings suggest that taking the motivational model by differentiating approach versus withdrawal emotions is more appropriate in the context of social media reactions. Likes on Facebook should be understood as an active way of attention-paying, because the motivational theory of emotion works better than the appraisal theory of emotion in predicting likes. Clicking like is more of an action, less of an indicator of careful cognitive evaluation of the messages.

With all the contributions, our study has its own limitations. First, our study focuses on the relationship between content features and user reactions on Facebook. We know that user reactions can be influenced by other factors such as the pages themselves, 23 the Facebook algorithm, and users' initial choice of following a page. Moreover, it is likely that there is a dynamic interaction among these factors when being linked to user reaction. Second, our analysis took likes as an indicator of attention-paying to align with the arguments of agenda setting. However, the analysis can bring more insights if we make use of other user reactions such as shares, comments, and emotional reactions. 22 Last but not least, the examination of the COVID-19 vaccine discussion on Facebook should be extended beyond the initial stage of shaping first impressions in the United States, and include later periods when the discussions evolved as well as other countries/contexts where the rollout of vaccines followed different timelines. Although the specific findings may differ from context to context, there is little reason to expect our broader qualitative findings to be undermined. In other words, we would still expect subissues identified from a corpus of social media data, and the sentiment encoded in their content can be indicative of user engagement with those posts on social media.
sections.text:
Our study focuses on a critical moment in fighting COVID-19 and Facebook public pages' discussions about COVID-19 vaccines. During this important time window to shape first impressions about COVID-19 vaccines, we found that first, Facebook posts talked about not only treatment effectiveness such as trial results and testing approaches but also other issues that surround vaccines such as approval and distribution. Second, although the general sentiment during this period was positive and anticipation was the highest emotion, Facebook posts expressed a substantial amount of fear and sadness, especially when discussing emergency approval and trial results. Finally, we found that both topics and sentiments had a significant influence on user likes.

Using the topic of vaccine distribution as a baseline, posts that discussed related aspects of vaccines (e.g., effectiveness, shipment, and testing) have received more likes, probably because of the additional information they provide. Posts that call for actions (e.g., use of masks) and indicate care for vulnerable groups (e.g., health care workers and seniors) received more likes, too. Unsurprisingly, bad news (e.g., new cases and deaths) and doubts over the usefulness of vaccines received fewer likes. The overall valence of sentiments does not have as much influence as the motivational direction of sentiments when predicting likes. Regardless of their valences, approach emotions lead to more likes, whereas withdrawal emotions lead to fewer likes. This finding is particularly interesting in the context of COVID-19 vaccines. During this critical window when the anticipation of vaccines was high, ironically anticipation, a withdrawal emotion, only decreased likes, probably due to the uncertainty in anticipating something that is yet to happen.

Other than its practical implications, our article contributes to the theoretical expansion of the NAS model by taking a network perspective on both the first-layer and second-layer agenda setting. In short, our analyses serve as the theoretical foundation on which the network nature of the NAS model can be better conceptualized and operationalized. We suggest that future studies on social media agenda setting should always take this network perspective in their analysis. In addition, our study helps us to theoretically understand the prediction of emotion theories in the context of social media. Traditionally, sentiments are understood as valences and their intensity. But our findings suggest that taking the motivational model by differentiating approach versus withdrawal emotions is more appropriate in the context of social media reactions. Clicking like is more of an action, less of an indicator of careful cognitive evaluation of the messages.

Second, our analysis took likes as an indicator of attention-paying to align with the arguments of agenda setting. However, the analysis can bring more insights if we make use of other user reactions such as shares, comments, and emotional reactions. 22 Last but not least, the examination of the COVID-19 vaccine discussion on Facebook should be extended beyond the initial stage of shaping first impressions in the United States, and include later periods when the discussions evolved as well as other countries/contexts where the rollout of vaccines followed different timelines. Although the specific findings may differ from context to context, there is little reason to expect our broader qualitative findings to be undermined. In other words, we would still expect subissues identified from a corpus of social media data, and the sentiment encoded in their content can be indicative of user engagement with those posts on social media.

However, the analysis can bring more insights if we make use of other user reactions such as shares, comments, and emotional reactions. 22 Last but not least, the examination of the COVID-19 vaccine discussion on Facebook should be extended beyond the initial stage of shaping first impressions in the United States, and include later periods when the discussions evolved as well as other countries/contexts where the rollout of vaccines followed different timelines. Although the specific findings may differ from context to context, there is little reason to expect our broader qualitative findings to be undermined. In other words, we would still expect subissues identified from a corpus of social media data, and the sentiment encoded in their content can be indicative of user engagement with those posts on social media.
